name: LIQUID PAGE - shader-dissolve multi-page transitions, each page a full distinct scene
ref: Codrops 'Building a Scroll-Revealed WebGL Gallery with Three.js and Barba.js' (February 2026, tympanus.net/codrops); multi-page WebGL transition portfolio pattern in Codrops Creative Hub 2026
palette: #070708 (void) + scene-specific accent per page: page 1 amber #e8b84b, page 2 cyan #00d4ff, page 3 coral #ff6b6b; transitions are the palette shift
brief: A multi-page SPA (index + 3 work detail pages) where navigating between pages triggers a full-viewport WebGL shader transition: a noise-masked dissolve sweeping diagonally that reveals the next page's color accent. Each page is a distinct full-scene composition (index is a minimal type list; each detail is a full-bleed cinematic spread) so the transition IS the experience of moving between visual worlds. Distinct from view-transition-morph (single shared-element morphs between index card and detail; same color world; no WebGL) and fold-origami (within-page CSS 3D fold; single page). One detail page link is pre-hovered at scroll=0. WebGL: avoid GLSL ES 1.0 dynamic-indexed local arrays (ANGLE returns 0); use a LUT uniform for noise thresholds; preserveDrawingBuffer:false; canvas paints before render loop starts. Mobile: WebGL transition replaced by CSS clip-path diagonal wipe. Guardrail: one Three.js canvas persists across routes, only geometry/texture swaps; DPR capped at 1; routing via native History API pushState. Three.js via importmap; NO GSAP or external libs.

name: PARTICLE SORT - works encoded as particles that self-organize into data-driven formations
ref: Codrops 'Particles Forming Images' (2025, tympanus.net/codrops/tag/particles/); generative-data-viz portfolio aesthetic in Godly 2026 data-art collection (godly.design/sites/)
palette: #050508 (void) + #e8f5e9 (ice-green particles rest state) + #ffcf6e (amber sort-target highlight) + #c0392b (vermilion active formation label)
brief: Each work is encoded as a particle cloud (100 to 200 particles per work, positions seeded from the image's pixel luminance grid). The full corpus of works floats as a galaxy of particle clouds in a Canvas 2D viewport. Clicking a sort button (by date, by color-temperature, by generation-time) fires a physics simulation: each particle springs toward its sorted-formation position using a simple attraction force, particles belonging to the same work stay loosely bonded, and the formation settles after ~1.5s. Hovering a cluster reveals the work thumbnail and metadata. Distinct from particle-constellation (a static star-map of work-nodes with constellation lines; nodes do not spring into data-sorted formations) and draggable-scatter (explicit drag-throw physics on card DOM elements, not particle swarms). The full galaxy of unsorted particle clouds is the scroll=0 state. Pure Canvas 2D; no WebGL. Mobile: particle count halved per work; touch triggers sort. Guardrail: total particle count capped at 2,000; spring integrator capped at 120 steps then snaps; rAF pauses when tab is hidden; reduced-motion skips spring animation (instant formation snap). Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: GOMMAGE - WebGPU TSL shader dissolves MSDF headline type into dust and petals on scroll
ref: Codrops 'WebGPU Gommage Effect: Dissolving MSDF Text into Dust and Petals with Three.js and TSL' (January 2026, tympanus.net/codrops/2026/01/28); WebGPU Three.js TSL workflow (Codrops WebGPU tag)
brief: Each work section's headline is rendered as MSDF text in a Three.js WebGPU scene; scroll progress drives a noise-threshold TSL shader that disintegrates the letterforms into amber petals and ice-dust particles, revealing the work asset beneath. The dissolve reads as a dramatic title card that self-destructs to show the piece. Distinct from dithered-lo-fi (WebGL Bayer-matrix dither on images, not text dissolve), fluid-xray-reveal (cursor-driven FBO fluid mask on images), and tech-organic-mesh (GLSL sine-displacement fragment shader full-viewport background). WebGPU: avoid GLSL ES 1.0 dynamic-indexed local arrays (ANGLE returns 0); use a LUT texture for noise thresholds; preserveDrawingBuffer:true; canvas sized to hero viewport only. Selective bloom via MRT. Mobile: WebGL fallback with a CSS clip-path wipe on the headline (no particle burst). Guardrail: particle count capped at 8,000; TSL compute dispatched only when hero is in view; DPR capped at 1 on mobile. Three.js via importmap; NO GSAP or external libs.

name: MORPH NAV - SVG path d-attribute morphs a living icon between states as the primary navigation
ref: SVG path morphing icon transitions (CSS Design Awards interactive-nav category 2026, cssdesignawards.com); SVG morph icon technique (Codrops SVG tag, tympanus.net/codrops/tag/svg/)
palette: #f8f8f5 (off-white) + #111 (ink) + #c0392b (vermilion active-state ring); icon geometry IS the color system
brief: The navigation is a single large SVG canvas where one glyph-icon (say a circle of dots) morphs its d-attribute to the next icon (a triangle, a cross, a ring, a grid) as the visitor scrolls between work sections; the current icon state identifies the section. Each morph uses a pre-authored keyframe sequence on the SVG path nodes (same node count, only coordinate interpolation, no external library) and is triggered once per IntersectionObserver crossing. Below the hero icon, a typographic section panel slides in. Distinct from proximity-glyph-field (a dense grid of a single repeated glyph reacting to cursor distance via weight axis; no shape morphing), morph (variable-weight headline letters reacting to scroll/cursor; full-letterform CSS variable font), and svgmap-journey (a journey route with waypoint unfolds; no icon morphing). The hero icon is mid-morph at scroll=0 so the mechanic reads immediately. All SVG fill/stroke are hex literals, not CSS var() in presentation attributes. No canvas; no WebGL. Mobile: same mechanic; icon scaled to 60vw. Guardrail: all morph keyframes pre-computed at load, never during rAF; reduced-motion freezes icon at each section's end-state. Native CSS/JS/SVG only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: PIXEL STUDIO - isometric pixel-art room you navigate to reveal works pinned to objects
ref: Awwwards nominee '1-bit Pixel Art Dev Portfolio' (awwwards.com/sites/1-bit-pixel-art-dev-portfolio); isometric pixel room creative portfolios (Godly 2026, godly.design)
palette: #0f0f1a (night-purple void) + warm neon pink #ff79a8 + cyan #7fefff + amber lamp #ffcf6e; 16-color constrained palette
brief: An isometric 3D pixel-art studio room rendered on a Canvas 2D grid of diamond tiles: desk, monitor, bookshelf, plant, record player. Clicking or hovering an object reveals the work pinned to it (monitor shows an image work; record player shows an audio-reactive piece; bookshelf volume opens a manifesto). Navigation IS object exploration rather than scrolling. Distinct from interface-nostalgia-os (Win95 flat desktop metaphor with draggable application windows; not isometric, not pixel-art), boot-sequence (CRT boot sequence revealing a gallery; passive, linear, no spatial exploration), and corridor-walk (first-person 3D ink-sketch hallway with scrolling movement; not isometric, not pixel). A lamp casts a warm cone of light and a ceiling fan rotates slowly at scroll=0 with one object already highlighted. Canvas 2D drawn from a tile-data JSON (no external sprite sheets; tiles drawn with fillRect and polygon primitives); click-hit testing via tile coordinate math. Mobile: pinch-to-zoom the canvas; tap objects. Guardrail: room re-draws only on pointer move (not rAF continuous); reduced-motion stops fan rotation. Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: CUTOUT REEL - full-bleed video plays only inside giant knockout letterforms, void everywhere else
ref: CSS background-clip:text + video knockout technique (CSS-Tricks; Codrops mix-blend-mode creative 2026, tympanus.net/codrops); knockout-text-over-video pattern in creative agency award submissions 2026
palette: #000 (absolute void) + video supplies all color; type weight and spacing are the only design decisions
brief: Giant uppercase words fill the viewport; behind them a muted looping video plays. The video is visible ONLY inside the letterforms via CSS background-clip:text on a background-image video-poster that swaps to a real <video> element using mix-blend-mode:screen on a white background, achieving the knockout without clip-path. Scroll advances the word displayed, each new word a new work title whose clip reveals a new video. Distinct from text-clip-reveal (background-clip:text with static images, letter-level parallax on hover; the image is visible through glyphs against a void background). This uses live video, absolute void outside letters, and mix-blend-mode, making the motion inside the type the signature. Words are a large slab-serif at 18vw minimum at scroll=0. Mobile: video replaced by a color-gradient matching the dominant hue; background-clip:text works identically. Guardrail: at most one video decoding at once (IntersectionObserver suspends off-screen videos); reduced-motion replaces video with a static poster frame. No canvas; no WebGL. All fill/stroke hex in any inline SVG. Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: PURE SCRUB - CSS animation-timeline:view() scrubs a multi-beat cinematic sequence with zero JS
ref: CSS scroll-driven animations view() timeline (Baseline 2024; Codrops scroll-driven animations 2026, tympanus.net/codrops); CSS @property animation-timeline:view() hero patterns (Smashing Magazine 2026)
palette: #0d0d0f (near-black) + #e8e0d0 (warm bone) + accent evolves cold #3a7bd5 to hot #e8472a as sequence progresses
brief: A tall single hero element (~500vh) whose animation-timeline:view() property drives a precisely authored multi-beat sequence entirely in CSS: phase 1 a headline types letter-spacing from 2em to 0 via @keyframes on scroll; phase 2 an image plate wipes in from a clip-path rectangle; phase 3 a color-grade filter moves from cool to warm via @property interpolation; phase 4 a CTA fades and scales up. Zero JS; zero IntersectionObserver; zero rAF. The technique makes CSS @keyframes scrubable by scroll position, a genuinely new post-JS primitive. Distinct from zero-js-scroll-timeline (six distinct works each with their own entrance animation; the mechanic is per-element entrance variety, not one sustained multi-beat sequence on a single element) and scrollytelling-chapter (sticky chapter rail + prose long-read with evidence inline; requires JS for chapter dot arcs). Phase 1 mid-play at scroll=0. No canvas; no WebGL. Mobile: identical (view() is mobile-supported). Guardrail: @supports (animation-timeline: view()) gate; fallback is static layout; @property declarations at :root; reduced-motion collapses all to instant opacity fades. Native CSS only, NO JS, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: TICKER MATRIX - a 2D matrix where each primary-row cell is itself an independent sub-marquee
ref: Marquee-of-marquees nested ticker layout (Awwwards experimental-developer category 2026, awwwards.com); studio kinetic-type marquee grids (Godly 2026, godly.design)
brief: The page is a 3x3 grid of cells where the primary grid itself scrolls horizontally as one macro-marquee, and inside each primary cell sits an independent secondary horizontal marquee of work thumbnails scrolling at its own randomized speed. The result is a 2D ticker: panning left-right across 9 cells while each cell independently streams thumbnails creates a kinetic field of motion depth impossible with a single ticker band. Clicking a thumbnail locks the macro-marquee and expands that cell full-screen. Distinct from diagonal-marquee-scroll (4 full-width bands tilted at +6/-6deg scrolling at a single speed-multiplied rate; no nested sub-marquee inside each band) and vertical-ticker (a single vertical broadcast-tape stock-crawl; one dimension, no nesting). The 2D ticker matrix fills the viewport already in motion at scroll=0 showing the two-level motion immediately. Pure CSS animation for inner marquees; JS controls macro-marquee speed. No canvas; no WebGL. Mobile: 2x2 grid; 2 inner sub-marquees. Guardrail: inner marquee animation capped at 12 cloned items; visibility:hidden on off-screen cells pauses their inner animation; reduced-motion pauses all marquees (static grid). Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: THE BOARD - a physical CSS 3D mixing-desk skin that IS the media player and gallery navigation
ref: Skeuomorphic design revival (Awwwards creative-interaction 2026, awwwards.com; Figma web-design-trends 2026); physical hardware UI skeuomorphism (CSS Design Awards interactive 2026, cssdesignawards.com)
palette: #1a1a1a (anodized steel) + #d4a84b (brushed gold knob caps) + #e03c2c (armed red) + #3a7bd5 (send blue) + #2a2a2a (matte channel strips)
brief: A mixing desk / control surface rendered in pure CSS 3D: channel strips with draggable faders (translateY on pointer drag), rotary knobs that rotate on drag (rotate() driven by pointer delta), a VU meter per channel animated with a CSS custom property, and a master fader that scrubs through work reveals. Each channel fader corresponds to one work; pulling the fader up reveals that work in a monitor screen above the desk. Pressing the red ARM button on a channel plays its media. Distinct from soundscape-reactive (Canvas 2D equalizer bars driven by Web Audio AnalyserNode; the aesthetic is a data-viz bar chart, not a physical mixer with draggable hardware controls) and ops-dashboard (a dark data-table operations console; software UI, not physical hardware skeumorphism). The desk is fully rendered with no scroll needed at scroll=0. No canvas; no WebGL; no external textures. All CSS gradients and box-shadow for material depth. Mobile: 2-channel simplified strip with one fader and VU. Guardrail: pointer capture (setPointerCapture) for fader and knob drag; reduced-motion removes animated VU (static bar at -18dB). Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: AMBIENT WEATHER - geolocation + Open-Meteo API drives a real-time ambient scene that wraps the gallery
ref: Weather-reactive ambient UI (context-reactive / API-driven design pattern cited in Index.dev web-design-trends 2026, index.dev/blog/web-design-trends); real-time environmental theming (Awwwards context-aware design 2026, awwwards.com)
palette: Dynamic: clear-day bone #f5f2ec + cerulean #6ab4e8; rain slate #2a3a4a + silver #9aaab8; storm charcoal #1a1a2a + violet #7a4ae8; fog lavender #c8c0d0 + chalk #e8e4ec; night ink #050810 + starfield white
brief: On load the page requests Geolocation (with a graceful deny-fallback to a default city) and fetches current weather from the Open-Meteo free API (no key needed). The WMO weather code drives one of five ambient scenes: clear, rain, storm, fog, night. Each scene is a full-viewport CSS gradient + SVG layer: clear has animated sun-ray SVG paths; rain has a Canvas 2D droplet curtain; storm adds lightning @keyframes flashes and rolling dark-cloud blobs; fog is a slow white CSS blur layer; night shows a star-field Canvas. The work gallery cards float above the scene unaffected. Distinct from time-of-day (a real-clock 5-band gradient driven by visitor's local time; no external API fetch; the signature is sun-angle and shadow-clock, not weather condition type). The ambient scene is live at scroll=0 before any scroll. SVG fill/stroke in hex not CSS var(). No WebGL. Mobile: Canvas rain curtain disabled; CSS-only equivalents. Guardrail: API fetch abort-controlled with 3s timeout; scene chosen from a WMO code lookup table (no dynamic index); reduced-motion removes all particle layers (static gradient only). Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: DATA TYPE - each letterform is a live SVG shape generated from the work's own metrics (time, cost, tokens)
ref: Generative-typography-from-data technique (The Pudding data-driven type, pudding.cool; generative-art typography in CSS Design Awards data-art 2026, cssdesignawards.com); data-encoded letterforms (Creative Applications Network, creativeapplications.net)
palette: #f4f0e8 (warm paper) + #111 (ink base) + per-metric hue: generation-time amber #e8b84b, cost teal #3ab8a8, seed violet #7b4ae8
brief: Each work's title is rendered as a custom SVG letterform whose stroke paths are algorithmically generated from the work's own metadata: generation-time encodes stroke weight, token count encodes letter width, seed number encodes a subtle jitter on each bezier control point. The result is a typeface unique to every piece; no two letters look the same across works. Hovering a letter reveals its metric legend. Distinct from morph (a variable CSS font whose weight/width axes react to cursor; the font is an existing typeface; no data encoding), variable-font-pressure (Roboto Flex weight pumped by cursor speed; a real font, not custom SVG paths), and proximity-glyph-field (a grid of a single repeated glyph, weight driven by cursor proximity rings; no per-glyph data encoding). The generated type for the first work fills the hero at scroll=0. Canvas 2D draws the SVG paths via Path2D; letterform control points computed from metrics at load. No WebGL. Mobile: letter count per title capped at 6. Guardrail: SVG path point count per letter capped at 40; paths computed once on load and cached; fill/stroke are hex literals, not CSS var() in presentation attributes; reduced-motion removes the jitter animation (static letterforms). Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: SCOPE CHAIN - CSS @scope proximity cascade makes nested component states ripple without JS
ref: CSS @scope at-rule (Baseline 2026, Smashing Magazine 'CSS @scope: An Alternative to Naming Conventions' February 2026; MDN @scope); @scope proximity cascade portfolio technique (CSS-Tricks 2026, css-tricks.com)
brief: A gallery of work cards organized into nested category sections (outer scope = category; inner scope = individual card). Hovering a card activates a CSS :hover rule inside its @scope block which, via @scope proximity, outranks an identical selector on a sibling card's outer scope — so the hovered card's inner scope overrides its own outer category color with amber while the category container shifts blue, and sibling cards in the same outer scope dim, all with ZERO JS visual logic. A second level: hovering a category header's @scope block dims work cards from OTHER categories via a scope-proximity gap rule. The interaction is a live demonstration of @scope as a cascade primitive. Distinct from has-relational-reveal (CSS :has() relational selector chain that propagates state across sibling cards sharing data-tag; the mechanism is relational pseudo-class not scope proximity) and magnetic-grid-snap (JS verlet spring physics; JS decides all transforms). @supports selector(:scope) gate; fallback is hover-only opacity. One card pre-hovered at scroll=0. No canvas; no WebGL. Mobile: focus replaces hover. Native CSS/HTML only; ONE small JS block for keyboard focus management only. NO GSAP or external libs.
